item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, man! Really glad to see that you enjoyed the main narrative and the multicoloured text! Also, about "containing Stonehenge," I didn't really want to delve too much into that, so I just wrote that the UNESCO and GOC were involved in the early stages, which would probably mean international travel and tourism laws, but I can definitely see why it might seem underdeveloped compared to the other parts of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man I love this! The tale element was particularly good, I love how the multicolored text works. Still not totally sure how well the concept of "containing Stonehenge" would work, since that seems like a huge very public operation that would for sure have people asking questions, but it didn't affect my enjoyment of the overall story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for replying! I was perfectly okay with waiting (all that WWS stuff must have been a nightmare to schedule) and I am very grateful that you still kept your word.

I shall attempt to improve the article (though I'll mostly use the Save Draft option as to not pile up the history list) and it might actually take 2 or 3 days (since midterms are such volatile goblins) (oh shoot, it actually took 5 days because my lazy bum can't organise a functional routine). I'll definitely use much of your advice on improving it, but there are some stuff I'm still unsure on changing, so let's talk about it.

Yeah, life's pretty busy
And quite the bitch, too (pardon the French)

I get what you mean to do with this "corruption" stuff, but it doesn't make sense given that we are reading an SCP document and that document can be edited at any time! Crossed out information simply wouldn't exist except via an infohazard that kept it there. These are live, updated documents. And since we don't get any even slight explanation for these corruptions until much later, they really pull you out of the experience on your first read-through. And probably on subsequent ones too.

I was trying to make it for constantly changing and incomplete actions done by certain entities, but I can see how it would be annoying. Infohazards might be a too straightforward way to explain it, maybe it's simply the nature of the beings? Fuck, I'm bad at this.

With such mental and spiritual experience, you would think he'd know it is does*, not "do", heheh.

Nyohoho.

What does the "animated factory" add to anything? It just looks really sloppy, messes up spacing, and also is a departure from tone.

I was referencing SCP-1004, though it might not be so hidden for computer monitors. I don't know, I never had time nor the resources to check.

[…] the cover story that the Incident was the result of several unaccounted solar flares damaging the ozone layers.

But… can solar flares do this…? I somehow doubt it.

Surprisingly, yes (though the results aren't as drastic as the Ansuman incident). It has apparently caused blackouts and changed the colour spectrum, even outshining the sun (if it pierces through multiple layers). The most extreme one was apparently a few years before the 20th century, a Carrington-sized flare.

Same goes for the introduction of "Doorhandle", and I do have something against such an obvious self-insert slash author-avatar. Just… hm.

Ah, shit. I'll try to make it work, as unlikely as it might be. Maybe with Tales or something, even if it takes forever.

Your flowers? They don't do this. Cut them.

I don't really know how to show its symbolism, so maybe I might do that. I have no idea anymore.

Your hostile entities? They take up far too much space when they eventually amount to "don't go near Stonehenge".

I don't know how I'd feel about dropping the slaves bit out. It might contribute just a little bit to the worldbuilding, but it does feel kind of useless outside of that. I'll see what my mind thinks of it.

Depictions and pictures of it having horrible effects? Adds nothing. Goes nowhere. Takes up a lot of space in your article. Cut it.

I'll see what I'll do about it.

Observation logs? This does nothing for us, we got this info from the description and we don't need a repeat that reveals so little.

Same as above, my mind holds no love.

And then, we don't even need an interview with Handel afterwards.

Well, I don't want dangle the big question of "How the fuck will the Foundation deal with him?" in the air, plus it's only for Level 5 personnel and above, so hopefully it doesn't hurt anyone except for me.

I think the letter at the end is rather unnecessary, especially because it wouldn't exist in this article in-universe.

I actually have some plans for that to come in play in-universe, mostly about his colleagues finally receiving information from him. Since he's currently the only one sent to the 11th level, him jumping back and forth between multiple levels of reality might jeopardize the mission.

That should be pretty much it! The ones that I didn't mention are the ones that will almost certainly be deleted and replaced. Once again, thank you for the advice! Changes should be done in a few days now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so sorry that I took 2 weeks exactly (14 days, hot damn), but I am a Butterfly Squad member, and I will not ignore a critique request. That is, unless it is an ideas thread critique request and by the time I get to it the concept has already been fully greenlit… but we don't talk about that. I will shoot you a PM to alert you to this comment's existence once I am done. Let us begin.

Life is a Busy One
Ain't It?

Line-by-Line / Technical Critique

In response of SCP-5536-3's anomalous properties […]

I spy description in the containment procedures. The conprocs don't need to tell us why. In fact, they are stronger if they don't, typically. Keep it strictly to what the procedures are.

You could probably benefit from defining a "trilithon" in a footnote — I had to look it up.

10-20 SCP-5536-2

Due to the dashes in the designation, I would write out "ten to twenty", or "10 to 20", because it confused me for a second.

Each of these flower bulbs measure around 30 cm […]

Measures*, as our object we are measuring is "each", which is singular.

[DATA EXPUNGED]

Umm… not only do I recommend against this use of expungement, I also think this looks really bad, and unless there comes a good explanation for it later I seriously suggest just making it black. But as for expungement, let me regale you with my usual spiel: if you the author can think of something cool/creepy that goes under an expungement, write it. If you can't, neither can your reader. And if you really want to keep something from the reader, there are slier ways to do it — usually involving the Foundation knowing a little less, or the Foundation omitting to mention data. Like, why should they even leave that part of the sentence in there, if they're just going to expunge it? They could as easily — or even more easily — just end the sentence earlier, not tease lower level readers of the document (in-universe, but also out-).

The redaction in the second footnote is more appropriate to the flow of the article because you're not removing potentially important information, you are making sure to keep up suspension of disbelief.

[…] orange point in the forehead region.

I think you can cut "region", it sounds very silly.

[…] requiring large amounts of explosives to properly be neutralised.

There are various forms of damage, "explosives" sounds very over-the-top, I would say "requiring excessive force to neutralize" (I would cut "properly" too, is there a way to improperly neutralize them?).

[…] seemingly limited to 30 meters away from SCP-5536 […]

I would say "seemingly limited to a 30 meter radius around SCP-5536".

SCP-5536-2 is currently believed […]

I think that should be are*, seeing as SCP-5536 is a collective designation for a group. Otherwise, I would say "SCP-5536-2 instances", seeing as otherwise you are describing one of them. So, how it is now reads weird. Please to fix.

[…] acting instead like a hive mind and following simple orders.

This could be phrased better. I suggest "acting instead as a superorganism with basic goals". I got distracted and had to come back, and I feel like I had a far better way to phrase it than that… but it's lost to me, and the point still stands. The "and following simple orders" is what rubs me wrong.

This effect would continue until SCP-5536-3 is removed from the exposure.

The last sentence was in present tense, so this should be "will" instead of "would".

[…] lasting usually between 7-8 hours […]

The phrase "lasting usually between" is clunky. I would say, "usually lasting from 7 to 8 hours".

After awakening, SCP-5536-3 would usually express discomfort […]

Another time we suddenly go into past tense. Be consistent.

SCP-5536-4 is a male human of unknown age and descent. It has currently shown no anomalous properties aside from its ability to safely emerge from SCP-5536. SCP-5536-4 claims to be an agent of GoI #1945 under the codenames "Dorren Handel" and "Doorhandle."

I respond with an emoticon: :/

More on this later.

As the MTF's members […]

I would make it just MTF*, no apostrophe, no possessive form.

[…] terminated […]

This once again looks awful.

[…] the cover story that the incident was the result of several unaccounted solar flares damaging the ozone layers.

But… can solar flares do this…? I somehow doubt it.

UNESCO officials would also prohibit any cultural and tourism activity on Wiltshire.

Why "would"? If I was an English major I could tell you the type of speech this is, but this is conflicting with your use of "was" before and after. I would replace "would" with "was" both here and in the next paragraph, and change sentence structure accordingly. The word "would" means "this did happen in the past, but it happened in the future in comparison to the point in time in the past that we are telling this story from". It's basically a way to say "this happens later, but we aren't moving there in this narration yet". Which is contradicted by using "was" later during these same descriptions, telling us "this is now the point of reference from which we are telling this story". It's very confusing, which makes it confusing to explain. But trust me on this. You want to change these to "was".

[…] the Foundation was finally given permission […]

"Finally" sounds strangely exasperated for clinical tone. I would simply cut that word.

When exposed to any ultraviolet radiation […]

This is rewinding a bit but I now have to mention this. You address in your containment procedures that non-UV lights have to be installed, and I looked it up, and you're right, all lightbulbs emit just a little UV light. Well, later…

On 24 July 2020, at 11:30 AM, as SCP-5536-3 exited the centre to greet her at the entrance, the area around him instantly combusted […]

What about the UV from inside the center, in the lightbulbs? Why is that a precaution the Foundation has to take if normal lightbulbs inside the center don't activate him? Is it a threshold? If it is, please say so.

SCP-5536-3 himself was slightly injured on the facial area, but received serious burns on his arms and torso.

"Facial area" is really silly sounding, and "serious burns" should be replaced with what degree of burns he got. You can look up what the degrees of burns mean, and choose what makes sense here.

[…] who was also given a cover story that her nephew had been immolated in the incident.

"Also" really interrupts flow here. I would cut the word.

The connection becomes even stronger due to the records showing that he was hit with its radiation right in its centre and was miraculously saved mere minutes later.

I… feel like I want more explanation. Also, your dialogue is not great, but I can go over that later.

Methods include shaking, shouting, blaring an airhorn, blowing a dog whistle, playing a high bass boosted JJBA non-descript media compilation, and tickling.

This is a very bizarre departure from tone. Like… this is just suddenly very silly. Honestly if you want to say "a loud meme compilation over the room's speakers", then just say that. That would still be silly, but it does sound like a thing they might do — just play something loud over the speakers. I dunno. This rubs me wrong.

SCP-5536-3 whispered "five months" before going completely silent.

This behavior was not cataloged under "what the subject does every time you ask him this question".

SCP-5536-3 started shouting in made-up vocalisations Wuski

You can't really "make up" vocalizations, but you can make up a language, so I would say that. Although I also think you could just do without the crossed out stuff… this is a digital document, would you in real life go through your Google Doc research project and cross out stuff to replace it with new stuff, or would you just change the information? I would say "started shouting in what was later discovered to be Wuski".

[…] radioactive particles light […]

Mmm.

You know what? Just one more test, and if this one also goes south, then it'll be the last one. - Dr. Natt

Very well. Do not make us think that we misplaced our trust in you, Doctor. - O5-█

Why would this correspondence be in the formal SCP document.

[…] instructed to draw a painting of SCP-5536.

You don't draw paintings, you paint them. But if you don't want to repeat "paint", then say "create", or "make".

Also, I have qualms about the results of this test that I think you can extrapolate from my previous complaints. But to be clear, why expungements, why cross-outs, why the weird bolding and colors? Why would these stay in the document? Also, "bursted to flames" should be "burst into flames".

I hope that you take more caution for your future actions, Doctor. - O5-█

Very well. Forgive me, Ma'am. It won't happen again. - Dr. Natt

This is a very unprofessional thing to just leave in an SCP document. See also: same question as above from the last instance of this.

[…] the floors falling backwards and the walls crawling through each other.

Same complaint as I had against SCP-3333; such flowery language would not make its way into an official report. Also, of the colors you have used, the ones here are the least intrusive. The red here looks nice, the light blue of "river" looks nice, but they still really need a reason to exist. Also, why the crossouts?

[…] those fucking redstone zombies!

Another strange out-of-place reference…

The colors chosen for Thorin and Handel look very nice.

If I do not ramble about it or say a bunch of numbers for hours, I'd be## [DA{Just kill me.}TA EXPU{Please.}NGED].

Frown.

This body of the child that I once was do not reflect my mental and spiritual experience.

With such mental and spiritual experience, you would think he'd know it is does*, not "do", heheh.

Dr. Natt motions her hand to the hidden camera.

Not terribly well hidden if she can just motion to it.

[…] anomalousanimated factoryporn?

Mmm.

Big Picture / Conceptual Critique

Okay I finished it. I also read all the other comments and your responses to them. Okay, so, this is a downvote from me, and I would like to explain why. Your pacing needs a lot of work — this is very long and the length does not feel justified for what it amounts to. Or, it could be justified, if only you focused on the more important pieces and expanded them more. Your dialogue is subpar though not abysmal — definitely salvageable (as is everything with enough work) — and I agree that a lot of this is quite "narmy", as TL333s put it. I also agree with basically everything cybersqyd said, and the colors did really irk me like they did for tupacofficial. But I don't think your basic idea is bad, really. I see hints of good shit all over this. Here is a bullet point list of other things I think I need to mention:

  • Colored text can look good. You accomplish this in a couple places. Colored text that isn't a single solid color is immensely harder to make look good than having words just be solid colors. And you definitely tried mixed colors. So I recommend you stop that.
  • You suddenly make joking references to other things at, like, a good many points. JoJo's Bizarre Adventure shows up in there, so does Minecraft, and wow those pull you out of the experience.
  • The way you hide all the hidden text is neither unique, nor good looking, nor actually hidden (except in the case of the "size 0" stuff, but then again that's nothing new and I would say it adds very very little). And, some of it would be best to just… not include? What does the "animated factory" add to anything? It just looks really sloppy, messes up spacing, and also is a departure from tone. Same goes for the introduction of "Doorhandle", and I do have something against such an obvious self-insert slash author-avatar. Just… hm.
  • This thing is full of unnecessary information. Refer to cybersqyd for the majority of this, and I will touch more on this later.
  • I get what you mean to do with this "corruption" stuff, but it doesn't make sense given that we are reading an SCP document and that document can be edited at any time! Crossed out information simply wouldn't exist except via an infohazard that kept it there. These are live, updated documents. And since we don't get any even slight explanation for these corruptions until much later, they really pull you out of the experience on your first read-through. And probably on subsequent ones too.

Okay, so, that out of the way, let's figure out what is important and create an article progression that really highlights it. So what happens is:

  1. Stonehenge becomes a portal between planes of reality.
  2. Agent Handel and Thorin's inhabitor both come through this portal.
  3. Handel works for some… higher-dimensional magistrate, tracking "prisoners" that have escaped. The relationship between Handel, Thorin, and the prisoners is rather unclear.
  4. Handel and Thorin come face to face, reach an agreement. Seems Thorin's "escape plan" is botched, as this body doesn't hold his powers together very well.

That is what I understood of it. There is so much information here that is entirely irrelevant, takes up oodles of space, and just follows the "rule of cool", which is a rule that states "if it is cool, it stays". Well, I think the rule of cool is not a good rule to live by. Let me restate it; the "optimal cool balance" states that if a part of your story can easily be made cooler, do it. If you do this, you will reach maximum cool. But it has to be part of the story first — it has to be part of something that contributes directly to the plot / characterization. Your flowers? They don't do this. Cut them. Your hostile entities? They take up far too much space when they eventually amount to "don't go near Stonehenge". The purple cube floating in the middle of Stonehenge? It does do this. It can stay. It takes up almost no space, and clearly visualizes the actual central anomaly (the portal). But the fact that Stonehenge emits UV rays? Well, it would be useful, if not for the hostile entities; you just need some effect that amounts to "Stonehenge is dangerous to approach", and radiation is a perfect way to do this — it makes thematic sense with the portal, it takes up less space in your article, it works as "thing that kills tourists when it activates" — it covers all the bases.

Other things that can be cut. Depictions and pictures of it having horrible effects? Adds nothing. Goes nowhere. Takes up a lot of space in your article. Cut it. Observation logs? This does nothing for us, we got this info from the description and we don't need a repeat that reveals so little. All the different activities split between the cycles of the sun and moon? Really not important except for the Lunar Eclipse when Thorin must be killed (plus, those are mostly about the entities and flowers, both of which I said can be cut). If you want a clearer connection to the sun and moon while taking up less space in your article, have the portal only be open at night, and note that its brightness seems to correspond with the cycles of the moon (full moon being full brightness). Done. So simple, very concise, much needed for this article.

You can cut down the recovery by a lot by just adjusting for my recommended cuts, making it about radiation and not going into the nitty gritty of where everyone went. Also, radiation poisoning is much easier to make a cover story for (and a solar flare wouldn't make Stonehenge "explode", so). The interview with Thorin right after his recovery… reveals nothing and isn't very well-written. I would cut it.

Oh, also, your description of what happens during Lunar Eclipses is already a bit nonsensical. I would just have it say "results in unpredictable temporal, spacial, and reality distortions", and then let the reader be curious, later letting the logs speak for themselves.

Also, Thorin's properties regarding UV radiation don't make much sense. They would make a lot more sense if you describe it as "contact with sunlight". The theme of night and day would be strengthened, and you wouldn't run into as many problems regarding the science of UVs as I pointed out in the technical critique. You should also mention in the recovery that Thorin was directly in the center of Stonehenge when SCP-5536 manifested, and so his survival is extra strange (so much radiation!) immediately. This only really came up later that he was right there — so I just thought he was "one of the survivors", not that he "made a miraculous recovery".

Okay, hhhhaaa… we're on to the actual Lunar Eclipse. This critique has taken me two hours so far. I would say, just make it the camera footage inside Thorin's chamber. You can have weird things be happening to the walls, the floor, et cetera. I think that if you were really moderate about colored words earlier on — like, blue for Handel, red for Thorin, and occasionally that orange for some influence by the Magistrate, but you'd have to keep it really simple, then you could get away with really capitalizing on that stuff here, and keep the majority of the colors in this interview. Not all of them. But most.

Like, maybe when the sun is mentioned, it's in the magistrate's colors. Stuff like that. But be really sparing about it.

Then we get here, we find out Handel was sent here by the Magistrate, Thorin's been inhabited by someone who has escaped the Magistrate through this portal, we discover that [whatever your explanation is for what exactly happened, because I am honestly quite lost, but I do think you really need to cut down some of the esoteric stuff — we need some kind of reference to why Thorin is running from the Magistrate (moon stuff), we need some reason why his connection to the portal is so strange (meaning why we have to kill him over and over), why keeping him here is the best option, like, maybe his botched portal and possession accidentally created a better prison than the Magistrate could put him in, and yaddah yaddah]. This would then become a moderately paced adventure about an interdimensional (or, inter-reality) fugitive that fucks up an escape attempt, followed after by an agent of the Magistrate, Handel, with heavy sun-and-moon imagery and theming. That. That would be a cohesive-as-hell article.

And then, we don't even need an interview with Handel afterwards. We could just have it be "Agent Handel is considered imperative to the containment of SCP-5536", or something like that, and that would be a strong ending, too. I think the letter at the end is rather unnecessary, especially because it wouldn't exist in this article in-universe.

God fuck that took a long time. But that's my critique. All over 3,000 words of it. Do with it what you will. Good luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for responding! Don't worry, I don't feel like getting dunked on. After all, I have summoned you to give your criticism on my finished product, regardless of how harsh it might seem.

Okay
Let's see

The ConProcs feel a little overwritten: they're mostly good ideas but there's a few details that might be best left assumed, e.g, the armed guards and the socialisation for good behaviour aspects1? I think the note about the lights being changed so they don't emit UV can also be implied from the mention of them not being exposed to UV?

I thought so too, but one of the early draft reviewers, Doctor Fullham, mentioned that there should be some consideration on how the Foundation keep him from exploding and treat him like a somewhat normal human being. So, I'm feeling a little split here.

Also, while you can get away with referring to subinstances (e.g, SCP-5536-2) in the ConProcs without defining them, you really should define what they are before mentioning what they do.

Yeah, sorry! Since the -2 instances don't really need any procedures (other than don't go near it), I figured that they probably shouldn't be mentioned in the ConProc. I might be wrong, and I usually am.

The Description is worse for this: you have 9 paragraphs of text just explaining what the anomaly is before you even kick off the story and it's too much.

I wasn't really sure how to describe 3 slightly connected anomalies with wildly different properties, so that was something I burdened myself with. I don't really have a clue on trimming them, for I am a man of wasteful words.

I feel like 5 pretty big paragraphs about what exactly happened to all the tourists around the SCP is a bit excessive and I'd really suggest trimming it down.

Perhaps. I'm not really sure how, though, because I may have put too many things happening in quick succession, so I just inevitably cornered myself once again.

The Observation logs feel unnecessary: they basically just reveal what the description already told us about the dreams, without adding any extra detail other than that the generic "astronomical events" are specifically "lunar and solar eclipses"; but that info is already hinted towards by the big focus on what happens during both of them in the ConProcs/Description?

The observation logs are mostly for going slightly in depth on the "long dreams" property, but of course, me being me, it ends up feeling bloated.

The Testing logs also feel a little unnecessary: there's some cool stuff mentioned in them but they don't feel…hugely important? At the end of the day, this is largely a story about extradimensional beings with a portal, and the testing logs mostly reinforce that the portal is dangerous to observe?

Cool stuff for the sake of having cool stuff. Always comes back to bite me every time.

The main problem with that is that I really don't have a feel for the stakes here? It feels like this is a thing where I should be acutely aware that the world is being balanced on a knife-edge because of, but instead it feels…abstract and not quite tangible enough?

Yeah, using partial dream logic is something I would probably never forgive myself for. Surrealism has never been my strongest card, so for that, pardon me.

The writing is super-patchy too: there's a lot of weird phrasings and iffy grammar and I really did attempt a line-by-line but it took me ages and I don't feel like that's the biggest issue here.

Some stuff are deliberate, like the words niatruc and éta répsax, others are just stupid pitfalls I keep on stumbling upon.

I am fully aware that the final addendum and the message to the Timmūs-Operative are the only decent saving graces here, but I'll see what I can do to improve it.

Thanks for the crit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[Noting I got a PM request to take a look]

This is…definitely a concept which has some really good ideas included in it, and there's some really neat world-building in here, but it feels…poorly executed?

[+] Weaknesses
– hide block

Uh, I hope you don't take this as me dunking on your SCP here: I'm trying to illustrate a whole bunch of stuff which doesn't work for me and by extension how it could be improved.

The ConProcs feel a little overwritten: they're mostly good ideas but there's a few details that might be best left assumed, e.g, the armed guards and the socialisation for good behaviour aspects1? I think the note about the lights being changed so they don't emit UV can also be implied from the mention of them not being exposed to UV?

The Description is worse for this: you have 9 paragraphs of text just explaining what the anomaly is before you even kick off the story and it's too much. It's generally boring to read, and it feels like there's perhaps a few too many anomalous things kicking about that don't have much importance (e.g, the bit where it turns stuff into 5 dimensional things; the secondary anomalous properties of the -3 instance; the plants as well: they feel symbolically important perhaps but they're over-mentioned and I don't quite get the symbolism).

Also, while you can get away with referring to subinstances (e.g, SCP-5536-2) in the ConProcs without defining them, you really should define what they are before mentioning what they do.

After this, there's a really large section about how this was discovered to kick the story off and it's…technically fine? I feel like 5 pretty big paragraphs about what exactly happened to all the tourists around the SCP is a bit excessive and I'd really suggest trimming it down.

The Observation logs feel unnecessary: they basically just reveal what the description already told us about the dreams, without adding any extra detail other than that the generic "astronomical events" are specifically "lunar and solar eclipses"; but that info is already hinted towards by the big focus on what happens during both of them in the ConProcs/Description?

The Testing logs also feel a little unnecessary: there's some cool stuff mentioned in them but they don't feel…hugely important? At the end of the day, this is largely a story about extradimensional beings with a portal, and the testing logs mostly reinforce that the portal is dangerous to observe?

I'm gonna take a quick formatting thing here: the yellow you use e.g, in [DATA EXPUNGED], and the blue you use in "river" are both…really hard to read? Like I get the colours are symbolically important and hint at who is saying what, but it really needs more contrast(?) with the white background for it to work (it might be worth checking out taboo here btw: it uses colour in a similar way and you can see how it's a lot easier to read because it sticks to pretty dark colours; but you also have the option to use different font styling as shown in the colour blind accessible variant of it).

Uh, everything after the end of the testing logs is generally pretty good and it's very much the strongest aspect of the SCP: unfortunately, you've buried it under a lot of cruft that's not very interesting. The main problem with that is that I really don't have a feel for the stakes here? It feels like this is a thing where I should be acutely aware that the world is being balanced on a knife-edge because of, but instead it feels…abstract and not quite tangible enough?

The writing is super-patchy too: there's a lot of weird phrasings and iffy grammar and I really did attempt a line-by-line but it took me ages and I don't feel like that's the biggest issue here.

Uh, I ended up downvoting this largely because while the core idea is good, the overall display is just…filled with too much stuff that didn't work for me, and the good doesn't feel good enough to overcome that :(

Footnotes
1. Well, I'd keep that second but I'd heavily trim it down

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's okay. Couldn't really help it. I got an idea and just ran with it straight to hell and back. Also, sorry if there's too much narm or word salad in there. Too much thinking about this kind of stuff really keeps me up at night.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Between the X-Man primary humanoid skip who's actually an extradimensional king(?) in disguise, the super-cryptic author avatar character who's also an extradimensional something in disguise, the gratuitous colored text, the crossed-out stuff that's almost universally in poor clinical tone, this is just narmy as hell, I'm sorry. You had me hooked with "something bad happened to Stonehenge", but that's really the only part of this I liked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks. I'm just glad that you found it somewhat enjoyable.

Yes, I probably need help with some of the language, given that English isn't my main dialect. The non-human interaction is probably better because I really like dark fantasy stories. Don't know if it will ruin the mystery, but I was trying to make Thorin (at first) 12 - 15 years old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The scope and ambition of this commmand enough repect to keep me from down-voting, but I would personally recommend that you seek some additional editing advice, for some tonal issues, but primarily for the organization and pacing of your containment and description sections. I felt like the interactions and dialogue between the two non-human entities were better written than the notes and dialogue of your scientists and researchers. I was going to say, too, that in the first interview with Thorin, it was quite hard for me to tell what age you were trying to write him as, but I suppose that's actually in line with his character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's okay. Sometimes I couldn't help dumping loads of information about a concept that I really like, so I'm mostly at fault for that one.

About the coloured text, I did it because I wanted to show what it would be like to go to different levels of reality. The text with different colours are for incomplete actions or transformations done by the entity doing so. I can understand that it might irk some people, so if you didn't enjoy it, that's perfectly understandable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little torn on this. While I enjoy the worldbuilding, as I made clear when greenlighting your pitch, I would have really appreciated if there was more of it done like the last addendum instead of in the form of exposition dumps in the interviews. There's also some issues with tone throughout, but seeing English is your second language, I'll cut you some slack.

I'm not gonna lie, the colored text really irks me. I guess this type of stylistic stuff works when used sparingly and with purpose, as in House of Leaves, but here it ends up being used excessively and seemingly without a method to the madness. Unless there's an actual reason as to why it's there, it comes across to me as nothing more than a gimmick. Also, readers are usually inclined to find patterns in stylistic deviations like this, so when it turns out there's none, it ends up being confusing and disorienting to read.

-1 for now, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, a little bit, because Taboo is one of my favourite articles, but I was also inspired by The Laundry Files and some King Crimson songs because I cannot escape the reach of JJBA's influence, plus some contemporary fantasy stories.

I wanted to make a setting that showed that the universe may suck and humans might not be able to make much of a difference, but they're not alone in their fight. It's like (as the Resurrection canon calls it) NobleBright.

Anyways, sorry if the reply sounded a bit clunky (English is my second language)! I'm very glad that you enjoyed what I have written!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the colored text. the reality warping. the worldbuilding in the last collapsible. i LOVE it, loved every second of it. a hearty +1. were you inspired by Taboo at all when writing this? i actually thought this may be where the SCP was headed.

item.title: Re: In the Court of The Crimson King?
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it was weird because I was thinking of writing an SCP with inspiration from the song. Guess you beat me to it. Anyways upvote.

item.title: Re: In the Court of The Crimson King?
item.content:encoded:
"The rusted chains of prison moons

Are shattered by the sun"


Heck yeah, my fellow human! I'm so happy that you got the reference! Some of the characters are also based on the song, if examined closely.

item.title: In the Court of The Crimson King?
item.content:encoded: Is this inspired by "In The Court of the Crimson King"? Just asking because many of the lines from this SCP are reminiscent of the lyrics. Heck, the title is very similar to the opening lyrics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, Ma'am! I'm very happy that you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This cool worldbuilding at the end is what makes me upvote. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's understandable. I was going for a new style that might or might not stick with some people, so if you don't like it, that's a perfectly reasonable decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As interesting as this is, reluctantly downvoting because I fundamentally do not get what they are going on about, why the text is the way it is, et cetera.

item.title: A Thanks
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! Really glad to see that you enjoyed it. I've somewhat improved it according to your advice. Hopefully I can get better as time goes on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what's going on, but unlike most other times wherein that's true, I'm absolutely loving this! The Magistereum is my new favorite GoI, that's for sure. Even if you don't get the -5000 slot (which, tbh I don't think is likely), then I hope you stick with this and write lots more!

I have only three sticking points -

1) Why would UNESCO call the Foundation, instead of the GOC? And why would the Foundation find out about this through them, rather than through watching the news, or social media?

2) This anomaly is very LOUD and public. Seems like a nightmare to contain, since it's at a tourist hot-spot. But, I couldn't find any consideration for that in the article. What gives?

3) I think this should be a Keter class, since Thorim could destroy the world in a few years, and because it's at a public landmark, and because containment seems pretty complicated (though apparently reliable).

Anyway, upvoted! Thanks for writing!

item.title: A celebration before the suns shatter the prison's rusty chains!
item.content:encoded:
We are the demons that keep the angels in check.

My 5000 contest entry and also my first SCP! Took me a few months to plan out, 2 months to write, and 2 weeks to fulfill the first-time-author requirements. Hopefully it's decent enough.

Big thanks to these amazing people:

  • Zyn and RockTeethMothEyes for reminding me of the forum rules.
  • Francis Scalia and tupacofficial for giving criticism about my idea.
  • Doctor Fullham and F15H35 for reviewing my draft.
Here are the pictures used in the entry: 
Part of Creative Commons (don't sue me, please)
  • The Stonehenge picture, created using art filters on a photo from Wikimedia Commons.
  • The existential kill agent, a drawing I made myself using kaleidoscope and rotation rulers.

The pictures are okay to use as long as credit is given.

For my alternate slots if I don't make the cut:
Here they are!
  • 5536
  • 5473
  • 5999
  • 5919
  • 5119

Thank you for everyone in this wonderful wiki!

- Doorhandle
